species: Gray Wolf Howl + Thunder Crack -- howl cut by lightning, high meadow, summer storm
scientific_name: Canis lupus (Gray Wolf, Order Carnivora, Family Canidae) + atmospheric lightning discharge (abiotic). Two-source event: biological vocalization interrupted by atmospheric transient.
sound_type: Howl cut by lightning. Temporal sequence: (1) Gray Wolf sustained territorial howl -- fundamental 150-400 Hz sweeping/modulating, harmonics to several kHz, duration 5-11 seconds per howl, amplitude 90-105 dB at 1m / 60-80 dB at 1 km. (2) Close thunder crack -- close lightning strike (<1-2 km), peak amplitude 110-120+ dB, broadband 100 Hz to several kHz, peak duration <0.1 seconds, followed by decay rumble 2-10 seconds. Thunder crack is 30-50 dB louder than howl at listener position; acoustically masks howl completely at 0.1 sec peak. NOT simultaneous continuous (like degree-332 polyrhythm) but temporally coincident: one cuts the other at maximum expression.
ecosystem: High meadow, subalpine zone. North Cascades and Washington Cascades, 5,000-6,500+ ft (1,524-1,981+ m). Above primary forest, open terrain, summer convective thunderstorm zone. Active Gray Wolf territory: Washington State 30+ packs as of 2024, primarily northeastern Cascades and Okanogan-Wenatchee region. Summer wolf behavior: pack at rendezvous site with pups, territorial advertisement howling at maximum annual frequency.
season: Summer storm. Summer convective thunderstorm: daytime orographic convection at Cascade elevations, typically June-August. Wolf summer behavior at maximum howling frequency (territorial advertising around rendezvous sites). The specific event requires: (1) wolf in high meadow at howling moment; (2) convective thunderstorm with close strike in same meadow zone. Not rare as individual components; rare as the specific coincidence of the two at the moment of the interruption.
family: Multi-species. Canis lupus (Order Carnivora, Family Canidae). Plus abiotic atmospheric lightning discharge (no taxonomy).
milestone: THE METAL SUMMIT'S MOST DRAMATIC NATURAL PAIRING. THE SPS ENGINE'S MOST NARRATIVELY DRAMATIC EVENT. Howl cut by lightning: biological acoustic at maximum expression (territorial howl, summer, open meadow) interrupted by atmospheric transient at maximum amplitude (close lightning strike, 110-120+ dB). E=9 through dramatic coincidence rather than sustained maximum (SPS-335) or frequency-floor depth (SPS-334) or full-spectrum breadth (SPS-333). The final SPS entry of the Metal Summit: maximum dramatic impact. Gray Wolf recovery narrative: absent from Washington for 70+ years, returning. The howl is an ecological restoration.

coordinates.description: Representative: North Cascades subalpine meadow zone, North Cascades National Park and surrounding wilderness, 48.5°N 120.5°W. Wolf territory: Okanogan-Wenatchee region, North Cascades. Summer thunderstorm zone: North Cascades convective activity June-August. Also: Selkirk Mountains subalpine zone for active wolf packs, Idaho/Washington border.

acoustic_data.source_1_wolf: Gray Wolf Canis lupus. Howl fundamental: 150-400 Hz, sweeping/modulating. Harmonics: multiples of fundamental to several kHz. Duration per howl: 3-11 seconds (single exhalation). Amplitude: 90-105 dB at 1m; 60-80 dB at 1 km in open meadow. Detection range: 10+ km under favorable conditions (cold calm air open terrain). Individual wolves identifiable by howl characteristics.
acoustic_data.source_2_thunder: Close lightning thunder crack. Lightning channel: 30,000 K in <1 ms; explosive air expansion. Thunder crack at <1-2 km: peak amplitude 110-120+ dB; broadband 100 Hz to several kHz; peak duration <0.1 sec; followed by 2-10 sec decay. Amplitude exceeds wolf howl by 30-50 dB at listener position: complete acoustic masking at crack peak.
acoustic_data.interruption_dynamics: The howl-thunder coincidence: wolf at 5-10 sec howl; thunder crack arrives mid-howl. Peak masking complete at 0.1 sec. During thunder decay, howl may be partially audible in reflective gaps. Wolf behavioral response: does not necessarily stop; may continue howling after thunder. The acoustic unit documented is the interruption moment, not the full behavioral sequence.
acoustic_data.e9_calibration: E=9 through dramatic coincidence: biological vocalization at maximum expression + atmospheric transient at maximum amplitude, temporally coincident, the second cutting the first. Individual sources E=6-7 (wolf) + E=7-8 (close thunder). Combined E=9 through the dramatic quality of the specific interruption event. Rarer than the individual components: requires close strike during sustained howl at maximum amplitude.

nodes.title: Howl Cut by Lightning -- Most Dramatic SPS Event
nodes.description: The specific acoustic event: Gray Wolf sustained territorial howl at maximum expression, interrupted by close thunder crack at 110-120+ dB. The biological and the atmospheric in dramatic temporal coincidence. E=9 through dramatic impact, not sustained maximum (SPS-335) or frequency depth (SPS-334). The Metal Summit's most narratively structured natural event.

nodes.title: Gray Wolf Recovery -- The Howl as Restoration
nodes.description: Gray Wolves absent from Washington State for 70+ years (extirpated ~1930 by predator control). Natural recolonization began ~2008. As of 2024: 30+ packs in northeastern Cascades and Okanogan. The howl in a Washington high meadow is an ecological restoration event. The sound was absent for seven decades. It is returning.

nodes.title: Subalpine Meadow as Acoustic Stage
nodes.description: High meadow above treeline (5000-6500+ ft) provides open acoustic environment for wolf howl propagation and direct exposure to convective summer thunderstorm. No forest canopy to absorb/scatter either source. Both the howl and the thunder arrive in full to the listener in the meadow. The habitat is the acoustic stage for the coincidence.

nodes.title: Thunder Crack Physics -- 30,000 K in 1 ms
nodes.description: Lightning channel heated to 30,000 K (5x solar surface temperature) in less than 1 millisecond. Explosive air expansion = shock wave = thunder. Close thunder (<1-2 km): peak 110-120+ dB, <0.1 sec, broadband 100 Hz to several kHz. The fastest large-amplitude natural acoustic transient accessible in subalpine habitat.

cross_references.relationship: E=9 + E=9 = E=18. The wolf's sustained howl, held and beautiful, cut by the atmospheric crack -- paired with AIC's held guitar-vocal harmonies, beautiful and dark, against the dissonant intervals that are always present in Cantrell's harmonic system. Both: sustained melodic expression against a background that delivers interruption or resolution at maximum drama.

Both involve Gray Wolf vocalization. Cougar-Wolf encounter (313): two apex predator species in proximity, territorial/threat acoustics. Wolf+Thunder (336): wolf territorial howl in atmospheric interruption. Same species, different acoustic contexts. Consecutive arc closures (313 cougar arc; 336 Metal Summit).

Both E=9 in Cascade high-elevation summer/storm context. Hurricane ridge wind (335): sustained maximum for hours. Wolf+thunder (336): maximum dramatic coincidence at a moment. The SPS Metal Summit arc: full-spectrum (333) to planetary deep (334) to sustained maximum (335) to dramatic instant (336).
